papers.title: Top brass in China reaffirm goal to be world leaders in tech, AI
papers.summary: China reaffirms its goal to become a world leader in technology and AI through a new five-year plan that emphasizes original scientific research.

papers.title: Author Correction: SLAMF6 as a drug-targetable suppressor of T cell immunity against cancer
papers.authors: Bin Li, Ming-Chao Zhong, Cristian Camilo Galindo, Jiayu Dou, Jin Qian et al. (+3 more)
papers.summary: SLAMF6 is identified as a potential target for cancer treatment, as it suppresses T cell immunity.

papers.title: Polymers with purpose: molecules can squirm free of the pack
papers.summary: Researchers discover that tightly packed molecular chains can move freely when energy is introduced by an enzyme.

papers.title: Briefing chat: ‘Can it run Doom?’ — why scientists got brain cells and a satellite to play the classic game
papers.summary: Scientists discuss the week's top science news in a briefing chat, including the use of a classic computer game for scientific research.

papers.title: How the classic computer game Doom became a tool for science
papers.summary: The classic computer game Doom is used as a tool for scientific research, including running it on bacteria and a satellite, and playing it by neurons in a dish.

papers.title: ‘RAMmageddon’ hits labs: AI-driven memory shortage is impacting science
papers.summary: A memory shortage caused by the soaring cost and limited supply of computer memory is impacting scientific projects, prompting researchers to find creative solutions.

papers.title: The problem with Canada’s plan to buy scientific prestige
papers.summary: Canada's plan to poach 100 US researchers may have economic benefits, but it could also harm the existing scientific community.

papers.title: Youthful antics predict lifespan — at least for these fish
papers.summary: Researchers have developed a 'behavioural clock' that can predict the lifespan of fish based on their activity levels and daytime sleepiness.

papers.title: Daily briefing: Vaccine-carrying mosquitoes could inoculate bats against rabies
papers.summary: Scientists have developed a new technique to prevent bats from spreading rabies by using vaccine-carrying mosquitoes.

papers.title: No such thing as a shark? Genomes shake up ocean predator’s family tree
papers.summary: New genomic analysis suggests that sharks may not be a natural biological group, with most species being closer to rays than to other sharks.

papers.title: ‘Einstein‘ bot sharpens debate over AI in the classroom
papers.summary: The 'Einstein' bot is being used to counter students who use AI to cheat in the classroom, highlighting the ongoing debate over the role of AI in education.

papers.title: Reckoning with my ‘ghost years’: why a low publication rate doesn’t always reflect failure
papers.summary: A researcher shares their experience of having a low publication rate, arguing that it doesn't necessarily reflect failure, and that different levels of output are acceptable.

papers.title: ‘Baked, not fried’: five highlights from nutrition research
papers.summary: A summary of recent nutrition research highlights the importance of potatoes, coffee, and protein in our diets, with some intriguing findings.

papers.title: How bioRxiv changed the way biologists share ideas – in numbers
papers.summary: An analysis of bioRxiv's first 13 years shows that the preprint server has become a major platform for biologists to share their ideas, with four million articles downloaded every month.

papers.title: Nervous networker or conference presenter? Just care less, says speech coach Susie Ashfield
papers.summary: A speech coach offers tips to boost confidence as a public speaker, suggesting that people should just care less about what others think.

papers.title: Author Correction: Gut stem cell necroptosis by genome instability triggers bowel inflammation
papers.authors: Ruicong Wang, Hongda Li, Jianfeng Wu, Zhi-Yu Cai, Baizhou Li et al. (+11 more)
papers.summary: This paper corrects the authors of a previous study on gut stem cell necroptosis, which was found to be triggered by genome instability and leads to bowel inflammation.

papers.title: This supernova is too bright – now astronomers might know why
papers.summary: Astronomers have found a possible explanation for extremely bright supernovae, which could be caused by a wobbling signal.

papers.title: This fish shouldn’t exist — the weird genetics of clonal vertebrates
papers.summary: Researchers have studied the genetics of the Amazon molly, a fish that reproduces asexually, and found evidence of what might be powering superluminous supernovae.

papers.title: The dynamic basis of G-protein recognition and activation by a GPCR
papers.authors: Kazuhiro Kobayashi, Kouki Kawakami, Toshiki E. Matsui, Shun Yokoi, Masahiro Fukuda et al. (+14 more)
papers.summary: Scientists have used cryo-electron microscopy to study how a G-protein receptor dynamically engages and releases different G proteins, revealing key mechanistic steps in activation and subtype selectivity.

papers.title: A large-scale coherent 4D imaging sensor
papers.authors: Francesca Fabiana Settembrini, Arif Can Gungor, Andres Forrer, Steven A. Fortune, Alessandro Dell’Aquila et al. (+8 more)
papers.summary: Researchers have developed a large-scale coherent 4D imaging sensor using a LiDAR focal plane array, which can obtain coherent images at high frame rates over useful distances.

papers.title: Capturing dynamic phage–pathogen coevolution by clinical surveillance
papers.authors: Yamini Mathur, Caroline M. Boyd, Jeannette E. Farnham, Md Mamun Monir, Mohammad Tarequl Islam et al. (+4 more)
papers.summary: Researchers captured the dynamic coevolution of a pathogen and its virus through clinical surveillance, revealing the molecular basis of natural selection.

papers.title: Multidimensional profiling of heterogeneity in supratentorial ependymomas
papers.authors: Daeun Jeong, Sara G. Danielli, Kendra K. Maaß, David R. Ghasemi, Svenja K. Tetzlaff et al. (+34 more)
papers.summary: Scientists identified two distinct progenitor-like cell states in supratentorial ependymoma tumors, reminiscent of early human brain development.

papers.title: Structures of Marburgvirus glycoprotein and its complex with NPC1 receptor
papers.authors: Gang Ye, Fan Bu, Hailey Turner-Hubbard, Morgan Herbst, Lanying Du et al. (+3 more)
papers.summary: Researchers determined the structure of Marburgvirus glycoprotein and its complex with the NPC1 receptor, revealing a distinct binding orientation.

papers.title: Multimodal electron microscopy of halide perovskite interfacial dynamics
papers.authors: Xinjuan Li, Qichun Gu, Wei Huang, Simon M. Fairclough, Richard H. Friend et al. (+3 more)
papers.summary: A new approach using multimodal electron microscopy enabled direct visualization of structural and chemical evolution in a working halide perovskite light-emitting diode.

papers.title: Lense–Thirring precessing magnetar engine drives a superluminous supernova
papers.authors: Joseph R. Farah, Logan J. Prust, D. Andrew Howell, Yuan Qi Ni, Curtis McCully et al. (+9 more)
papers.summary: Observations of a nearby supernova provided evidence of a centrally located magnetar surrounded by an infalling accretion disk undergoing Lense–Thirring precession.

papers.title: Facile induction of immune tolerance by an interleukin-2–TGFβ surrogate agonist
papers.authors: Qinli Sun, Alison K. Barrett, Masato Ogishi, Huiyun Lyu, Hua Jiang et al. (+9 more)
papers.summary: A fusion protein that combines IL-2 and a TGFβ mimic can induce immune tolerance by activating IL-2 and TGFβ signalling pathways in T cells, leading to the creation of regulatory T cells.

papers.title: Snapshots of the dynamic basis of NTSR1 G protein subtype promiscuity
papers.authors: Alina A. Vo, Arnab Modak, Sumin Lu, Scott C. Blanchard, Nevin A. Lambert et al. (+1 more)
papers.summary: Researchers used cryo-electron microscopy to study the activation of G protein subtypes bound to NTSR1, revealing structural motifs that stabilize transient complexes along the activation pathway.

papers.title: Intestinal interoceptive dysfunction drives age-associated cognitive decline
papers.authors: Timothy O. Cox, Ashwarya S. Devason, Alan de Araujo, Sydney Mason, Madhav Subramanian et al. (+43 more)
papers.summary: Age-related changes in the gut microbiome lead to cognitive decline by increasing inflammation and impairing vagal signalling, but targeting this gut-brain pathway can restore memory in aged mice.

papers.title: Assembly of helper NLR resistosome clusters upon activation of a coiled-coil NLR
papers.authors: Dongdong Ge, Fausto Andres Ortiz-Morea, Yingpeng Xie, In-Cheol Yeo, Qiaochu Shen et al. (+5 more)
papers.summary: A coiled-coil NLR called SUMM2 promotes the assembly of higher-order resistosome clusters to initiate cell death in plants.

papers.title: A sorghum pangenome reference improves global crop trait discovery
papers.authors: Geoffrey P. Morris, Avril M. Harder, Adam L. Healey, Chloee M. McLaughlin, Joanna L. Rifkin et al. (+70 more)
papers.summary: A pangenome reference for sorghum aims to accelerate crop breeding by providing a comprehensive understanding of the crop's genetic diversity and phenotypic traits.

papers.title: B cell imprinting in children impairs antibodies to the haemagglutinin stalk
papers.authors: Jiayi Sun, Gyunghee Jo, Chloe A. Troxell, Yanbin Fu, Robert Hoezl et al. (+32 more)
papers.summary: B cell imprinting in children caused by influenza viruses can lead to a shift in the immune response against key epitopes, impairing antibody production.

papers.title: Nanophotonic waveguide chip-to-world beam scanning
papers.authors: Matt Saha, Y. Henry Wen, Andrew S. Greenspon, Matthew Zimmermann, Kevin J. Palm et al. (+12 more)
papers.summary: A nanophotonic waveguide chip-to-world beam scanning technology has been developed, enabling scalable and compact 2D beam scanning with applications in displays, sensing, and quantum photonics.

papers.title: A big-push community intervention reduced rates of child marriage by 80%
papers.authors: Isabelle Cohen, Maryam Abubakar, Daniel Perlman
papers.summary: A community-wide intervention in northern Nigeria reduced rates of child marriage from 79% to 14%, demonstrating the effectiveness of bundled, big-push approaches in shifting entrenched behaviors.

papers.title: Immune evasive DNA donors and recombinases license kilobase-scale writing
papers.authors: Connor J. Tou, Keqiang Xie, Joana Ferreira da Silva, Pazhanichamy Kalailingam, Eliz Amar-Lewis et al. (+16 more)
papers.summary: A new DNA delivery method called INSTALL has been developed, enabling kilobase-scale integration strategies without the need for viral vectors, by synergizing immune-stealth nucleic acids with recombinases.

papers.title: Natural maternal immunity protects neonates from Escherichia coli sepsis
papers.authors: Raymond E. Diep, Ujjwal Adhikari, Kubra Gokce Tezel, Giang Pham, Allison R. Burrell et al. (+10 more)
papers.summary: Maternal preconceptual colonization with probiotic E. coli can prevent neonatal sepsis caused by Escherichia coli in a mouse model, by transferring pathogen-specific maternal antibodies to the neonate.

papers.title: Ageing promotes metastasis via activation of the integrated stress response
papers.authors: Angana A. H. Patel, Jozefina J. Dzanan, Kevin X. Ali, Ella A. Eklund, Samantha W. Alvarez et al. (+24 more)
papers.summary: Ageing promotes metastasis in cancer by activating the integrated stress response, which could lead to new treatments for older patients.

papers.title: Risk-adaptive therapy guided by dynamic ctDNA in nasopharyngeal carcinoma
papers.authors: Jiawei Lv, Dan-Xue Zheng, Jin-Hui Liang, Ning Zhang, Zu-Lu Ye et al. (+37 more)
papers.summary: Monitoring ctDNA clearance during treatment for nasopharyngeal cancer can help guide therapy decisions and improve patient outcomes.

papers.title: Gene conversion empowers natural selection in a clonal fish species
papers.authors: Edward S. Ricemeyer, Nathan K. Schaefer, Kang Du, Irene da Cruz, Susanne Kneitz et al. (+6 more)
papers.summary: Gene conversion helps a clonal fish species maintain a diverse mutational landscape and evade functional decay, revealing a mechanism for natural selection.

papers.title: A mechanism to initiate emergency type 2 myelopoiesis
papers.authors: Alexandre Fagnan, Cristina Di Genua, Yiran Meng, Roy Drissen, Zishan Zhang et al. (+6 more)
papers.summary: A new mechanism for initiating emergency type 2 myelopoiesis in response to parasitic worm infection has been discovered.

papers.title: Daily briefing: ‘Virtual cell’ simulates nearly every chemical reaction in the real thing
papers.summary: A new simulation models nearly every chemical reaction in a virtual bacterial cell, providing a powerful tool for understanding cellular processes.

papers.title: Memory loss is fuelled by gut microbes in ageing mice
papers.summary: A study in mice found that gut microbes contribute to memory loss as the mice age. This discovery could lead to new therapies targeting the gut to reverse cognitive decline.

papers.title: China pledges billion-dollar spending boost for science
papers.summary: China plans to increase funding for national laboratories and research projects by a billion dollars.

papers.title: What is the science behind ‘science-backed’ supplements?
papers.summary: A study found that decoding the research behind health supplements is often difficult due to the complexity of the research.

papers.title: Ultra-bright supernova wobbles like a spinning top
papers.summary: A supernova was found to be powered by a magnetized neutron star that causes it to oscillate in brightness.

papers.title: How an all-female fish species defies evolutionary expectations
papers.summary: A study of an all-female fish species found that asexual reproduction can be successful due to genetic 'copy and paste' mechanisms.

papers.title: Quantum entanglement as a tool to image distant astronomical objects
papers.summary: Researchers demonstrated entanglement-assisted interferometry, a technique that uses quantum entanglement to improve the sensitivity of long-baseline interferometers. This breakthrough could lead to more accurate observations of distant astronomical objects.

papers.title: Physics at risk: UK science leader on what’s wrong with the latest funding cuts
papers.summary: The UK's recent funding cuts for science may lead to a loss of the next generation of scientists, according to the president of the Institute of Physics. He calls for constructive dialogue with the government to address the issue.

papers.title: Using mosquitoes to vaccinate bats could curb the spread of deadly diseases
papers.summary: Scientists propose using mosquitoes to vaccinate bats against deadly diseases, but there are practical and ethical challenges to overcome before this strategy can be deployed.

papers.title: Photonic ‘ski jump’ steers light beam from silicon chip
papers.summary: Researchers developed a photonic 'ski jump' technique that uses tiny electrically controlled cantilevers to steer light beams from a silicon chip. This innovation could improve beam scanners.

papers.title: A silicon chip that enables the creation of 4D cameras
papers.summary: Scientists created a silicon chip that enables the creation of 4D cameras, which can map the position and velocity of 3D objects in a dynamically evolving scene.

papers.title: Dynamics of circulating tumour DNA can guide treatment decisions for improved outcomes
papers.summary: A phase II trial found that circulating tumour DNA can guide treatment decisions to improve cancer outcomes.

papers.title: Statistics reach a 'crisis point': nations struggle with a critical lack of data
papers.authors: Ehsan Masood, Subhra Priyadarshini, Lucila Pinto
papers.summary: Researchers are sounding the alarm over the lack of official data sets in the United States, Argentina, the United Kingdom, and India.

papers.title: Universities have become property businesses. What does that mean for research?
papers.summary: Universities in the United States, United Kingdom, Australia, and Singapore are operating as property businesses, raising questions about the role of science.

papers.title: AI can ‘same-ify’ human expression — can some brains resist its pull?
papers.summary: Emerging evidence suggests that LLM outputs can shape the text and thoughts of human users, raising questions about the potential impact on human expression.

papers.title: A fellowship of the rings in plant defence
papers.summary: Infected plants assemble immune-protein complexes on cell membranes to induce defence responses and cell death, forming large ring-like clusters.

papers.title: Child marriages plunged when girls stayed in school in Nigeria
papers.summary: A collaboration between researchers and religious leaders in Nigeria led to an 80% decrease in the likelihood of early marriage among girls who stayed in school.

papers.title: ‘Black rain’ in Tehran — what are the health effects?
papers.summary: Toxic smoke from burning oil depots in Tehran has blanketed the city, posing health risks to residents.

papers.title: Gut microbes affect cognition during ageing
papers.summary: Changes in gut microorganisms contribute to cognitive decline in aging mice by altering signaling between the gut and brain.

papers.title: Marriage of adolescent girls in Nigeria reduced by 80% by ‘big push’ intervention
papers.authors: Isabelle Cohen, Maryam Abubakar, Daniel Perlman
papers.summary: A locally tailored intervention in northern Nigeria reduced the rate of marriage among adolescent girls from 86% to 21%.

papers.title: Scientists revive activity in frozen mouse brains for the first time
papers.summary: Researchers have revived activity in frozen mouse brains for the first time, bringing 'cryosleep' closer to reality.

papers.title: Maximizing carrier extraction in hybrid back-contact silicon solar cells
papers.authors: Zilong Zheng, Xiqi Yang, Jiaxing Wang, Qinghua Zeng, Chaohua Zhang et al. (+17 more)
papers.summary: Researchers developed a method to improve carrier extraction in hybrid back-contact silicon solar cells, which could increase their efficiency.

papers.title: Alcohol group migration by proximity-enhanced H atom abstraction
papers.authors: Qian Xu, Yichen Nie, Jacob-Jan Haaksma, Ronghua Zhang, Natalie Holmberg-Douglas et al. (+5 more)
papers.summary: Scientists discovered a new mechanism for alcohol group migration through proximity-enhanced H atom abstraction, which could have implications for organic chemistry.

papers.title: Artificial general intelligence must be assessed in its scientific and societal context
papers.summary: The author argues that artificial general intelligence should be evaluated in its scientific and societal context to understand its potential benefits and risks.

papers.title: Prevent pandemics through One Health commitments
papers.authors: Marisa Peyre, Denise Rabold, Nina Jamal, Stella Chungong
papers.summary: The authors propose that One Health commitments can help prevent pandemics by promoting a holistic approach to human, animal, and environmental health.

papers.title: How ‘Pele’s hair’ sprouts from erupting lava
papers.summary: Experiments with simulated magma showed that it can be pulled into long strands similar to those seen in lava tubes, known as 'Pele's hair'.

papers.title: The ethical risks of open-access agreements being used for authorship leverage
papers.authors: Giovanni Spitale, Federico Germani, Nikola Biller-Andorno
papers.summary: Summary unavailable.

papers.title: Could flies sniff out contraband chemicals?
papers.summary: Summary unavailable.

papers.title: Privilege, power and vulnerability in science: precarious funding can prompt unethical ties
papers.authors: Joana Ribeiro, Luís Reino, Pedro Bingre
papers.summary: Summary unavailable.

papers.title: Live parrots were carried across the Andes before the Incas’ rise
papers.summary: Summary unavailable.

papers.title: Keep calm and be transparent: advice from scientists who retracted their papers
papers.summary: Summary unavailable.

papers.title: How data can help to guide NIH funding policy
papers.summary: Summary unavailable.

papers.title: Daily briefing: A daily multivitamin slows the signs of biological ageing
papers.summary: Summary unavailable.

papers.title: Forget SkinTok: the real science of skincare and why it matters for your health
papers.summary: Summary unavailable.

papers.title: Stop the use of AI in war until laws can be agreed
papers.summary: Summary unavailable.

Young, Mark Estacion, Yueqi Zhang, José Manuel Ruiz-Jiménez, Anandita Nadkarni, Victor Luria, Suvimal Kumar Sindhu, Ipsita Chatterjee, Akemi Shibata, Dan Liang, Hyesun Cho, Saejeong Park, Ana Spajic, Rothem Kovner, Martina Glavan, Rachel J. Chen, Ryan D. Risgaard, Xinyun Li, Sirisha Pochareddy, Amir Karger, Anita Huttner, Yury M. Morozov, Etienne W. Daadi, Carlo Colantuoni, Kevin T. Gobeske, John J. Ely, Patrick R. Hof, Marcel M. Daadi, Chet C. Sherwood, Alvaro Duque, Shaojie Ma, Andre M.M. Sousa, Stephen G. Waxman, Pasko Rakic, Gabriel Santpere, Stephan J. Sanders, Nenad Sestan
papers.summary: The human cerebellum has unique features compared to other primates, and the protein ZP2 plays a key role in regulating synapse development. ZP2 expression is induced by pontine mossy fibers, leading to decreased synaptic proteins and neuronal activity.

papers.title: Respiratory viral infections prime accelerated lung cancer growth
papers.authors: Wei Qian, Xiaoqin Wei, Andrew J. Barros, Xiangyu Ye, Haibo Zhang, Qing Yu, Samuel P. Young, Eric V. Yeatts, Yury Park, Chaofan Li, Sijie Hao, Gislane Almeida-Santos, Jinyi Tang, Harish Narasimhan, Nicole A. Kirk, Valeria Molinary, Ying Li, Li Li, Bimal N. Desai, Peter Chen, Kwon-Sik Park, Anny Xiaobo Zhou, Jeffrey M. Sturek, Wei Chen, In Su Cheon, Jie Sun
papers.summary: Severe COVID-19 can increase the risk of lung cancer, and viral pneumonia can induce epigenetic changes that promote tumor growth. These changes can be reversed with combined CXCR2 inhibition and PD-L1 blockade.

papers.title: Pluripotent stem-cell-based screening uncovers sildenafil as a mitochondrial disease therapy
papers.authors: Annika Zink, Dao-Fu Dai, Annika Wittich, Marie-Thérèse Henke, Giulia Pedrotti, Sonja Heiduschka, Guillem Santamaria, Tancredi Massimo Pentimalli, Christian Brueser, Sofia Notopoulou, Abdul Rahim Umar, Aleksandra Zhaivoron, Laura Petersilie, Caleb Jerred, Jesper Bergmans, Louis Anton Neu, Fabian Schumacher, Jan Keller-Findeisen, Agnieszka Rybak-Wolf, Daniel Stach, Jeanette Reinshagen, Undine Haferkamp, Kim Krieg, Andrea Zaliani, Liliya Euro, Alessia Di Donfrancesco, Chiara Santanatoglia, Enrica Cappellozza, Marta Suarez Cubero, Mario Pavez-Giani, Oleh Bakumenko, David Meierhofer, Alan Foley, Susanne Morales-Gonzalez, Isabella Tolle, Diran Herebian, Daniele Bonesso, Giulia Cecchetto, Sakurako Nagumo Wong, Monica Moresco, Alessandra Maresca, Ilaria Decimo, Francesco De Sanctis, Annalisa Adamo, Merel J.W. Adjobo-Hermans, Roberto Duchi, Maria Barandalla, Marco Scaglia, Andrea Perota, Cesare Galli, Burkhard Kleuser, Lukas Cyganek, Chris Mühlhausen, Lars Schlotawa, Valeria Tiranti, Ertan Mayatepek, Ildiko Szabo, Chiara La Morgia, Thomas Klopstock, Valerio Carelli, Felix Distelmaier, Andrea Rossi, Nikolaus Rajewsky, Ghanim Ullah, Stefan Jakobs, Christine R. Rose, Spyros Petrakis, Frank Edenhofer, Werner J.H. Koopman, Pawel Lisowski, Anu Suomalainen, Dario Brunetti, Antonio del Sol, Emanuela Bottani, Ole Pless, Markus Schuelke, Alessandro Prigione
papers.summary: A screening study using pluripotent stem cells identified sildenafil as a potential therapy for mitochondrial disease. Sildenafil extended lifespan in mammalian models and improved symptoms in individuals with Leigh syndrome.

papers.title: Papuan admixture predated the settlement of Palau
papers.authors: Yue-Chen Liu, Joanne Eakin, Jolie Liston, Rosalind Hunter-Anderson, Calvin Emesiochel, Kiblas Soaladaob, Sunny O. Ngirmang, Olivia Cheronet, Carla S. Hadden, Alexander Cherkinsky, Matthew Spriggs, Keith M. Prufer, Swapan Mallick, Nadin Rohland, Ron Pinhasi, David Reich
papers.summary: Ancient DNA data from Palau reveal that the island was settled by individuals with Papuan admixture more than 3,200 years ago. This admixture was present among the ancestors of the first settlers.

papers.title: Microbiome-produced nicotinic acid controls colon regional identity and injury susceptibility
papers.authors: Jérémie Rispal, Jasmine R. Garcia, Brisa Palikuqi, Manasa Vegesna, Dedeepya Vaka, Seung Woo Kang, Coralie Trentesaux, Juan Du, Nicola R. Realini, Paige N. Spencer, James M. Gardner, Annika Hausmann, Michael G. Kattah, Ken S. Lau, Dario Boffelli, Ophir D. Klein
papers.summary: The gut microbiome produces nicotinic acid, which controls regional identity and injury susceptibility in the colon. This extrinsic signal is crucial for maintaining tissue homeostasis.

papers.title: In vivo CAR-Tfh cell reprogramming restores tolerance in a mouse model of autoimmune hepatitis
papers.authors: Zuolin Zheng, Ying Liu, Ganyu Wang, Xinyuan Li, Shengxuan Peng, Zhen Jia, Xiao Yue, Zhipeng Fu, Xiaotian Zhao, Maosen Han, Zhenmei Yang, Chunwei Tang, Jinxin Gao, Zhichao Kong, Chen Chen, Kun Zhao, Tao Li, Xinyi Jiang, Weiqiang Jing
papers.summary: Researchers developed a method to reprogram T cells into regulatory cells to treat autoimmune hepatitis in mice. This approach restored liver immune tolerance and could be a new treatment for autoimmune diseases.

papers.title: Cellular immunotherapy for COVID-19-induced acute respiratory distress syndrome: Results of the CIRCA-19 phase 1 safety and phase 2 randomized controlled trials
papers.authors: Shane W. English, Dean A. Fergusson, Manoj Mathew Lalu, David W. Courtman, Saad Khan, Mohamad Sobh, Irene Watpool, Josee Champagne, Samantha Hodgins, Bernard Thébaud, Karim Soliman, Michaël Chassé, Claudia C. dos Santos, Marius A. Möbius, Daniel Freund, Mario Rüdiger, Duncan J. Stewart
papers.summary: A phase 1 and 2 clinical trial found that mesenchymal stromal cells improved outcomes for patients with COVID-19-induced respiratory distress syndrome. Patients who received the treatment had better quality of life and reduced need for breathing support.

papers.title: Nuclear Lipin1 recruits HDAC2 to epigenetically repress SREBP1-dependent lipid synthesis and myelination in hypoxic preterm white matter injury
papers.authors: Xinyu Li, Xiaoyu Yao, Chunjie Guo, Yilin Liu, Meiting Liu, Jing Li, Meng Zhang, Yu Feng, Chao Ren, Ruiqin Yao
papers.summary: Researchers discovered that Lipin1 recruits HDAC2 to epigenetically repress lipid synthesis and myelination in preterm white matter injury. Disrupting the Lipin1-HDAC2 axis restored myelination in preterm white matter injury models.

papers.title: New legal category of ‘advanced regenerative medicine treatment’ in Korea’s amended regenerative medicine law: comparative lessons from Japan
papers.authors: Jinah Yoon, Sukhyang Lee, Jung-Hyun Kim
papers.summary: South Korea's amended regenerative medicine law created a new category of 'advanced regenerative medicine treatment'. This article compares the new category to Japan's framework and discusses patient access, review processes, and safety monitoring.

papers.title: A logic-gated trispecific engager enhances macrophage killing of cancer cells in solid tumors
papers.authors: Xiaotian Zhao, Weiqiang Jing, Ganyu Wang, Zhanyan Liu, Xinxin Xu et al. (+14 more)
papers.summary: Researchers developed a trispecific engager that enhances macrophage killing of cancer cells in solid tumors. This approach could be a new treatment for cancer.

papers.title: Biomolecular profiling for noninvasive health monitoring
papers.authors: Moon-Ju Kim, José A. Lasalde-Ramírez, Wenzheng Heng, Wei Gao
papers.summary: Researchers developed a wearable sensor and mass spectrometry system for noninvasive health monitoring.

papers.title: Quantifying endosomal escape in vivo to guide lipid nanoparticle design
papers.summary: A lysosomal barcoding strategy was used to quantify endosomal escape of nucleic acids in vivo, improving lipid nanoparticle design.

papers.title: In vivo endosomal escape assay identifies mechanisms for efficient hepatic LNP delivery
papers.authors: Antony Jozić, Chloé Le Roux, Jeonghwan Kim, Mathieu Berchel, Deepak Kumar Sahel et al. (+10 more)
papers.summary: An in vivo endosomal escape assay was developed to optimize lipid nanoparticle delivery to the liver.

papers.title: Engineered TnpB genome editors for plants and human cells identified by ribonucleoprotein mutational scanning
papers.authors: Brittney W. Thornton, Rachel F. Weissman, Jorge E. Rodriguez, Ryan V. Tran, Brenda T. Duong et al. (+12 more)
papers.summary: Engineered TnpB genome editors were developed for improved genome editing in plants and human cells.

papers.title: The promises and challenges of neoantigen cancer vaccines
papers.summary: A review of neoantigen cancer vaccine development highlights areas for further technological advances and optimized trial design.

papers.title: B cell depletion in autoimmune disease: in vivo CAR-T or T cell engagers?
papers.summary: Researchers compared CAR-T cells and T cell engagers for B cell depletion in autoimmune diseases, finding that CAR-T cells are a promising option.

papers.title: Bringing magnetofluorescent proteins into the cell
papers.summary: Scientists developed a method to bring magnetofluorescent proteins into cells, enabling new applications in cell biology.

papers.title: Engineering gut biosensors
papers.summary: Researchers engineered gut biosensors to monitor gut health and disease, with potential applications in personalized medicine.

papers.title: Live imaging of neuronal dynamics in transparent mouse brains
papers.summary: Scientists developed a new optical clearing medium, SeeDB-Live, to make live mammalian tissues transparent for imaging experiments.

papers.title: An update on spatial proteomics
papers.summary: Researchers provided an update on the latest developments in spatial proteomics, highlighting advances in the field.

papers.title: Learning genes deeply
papers.summary: Annevo uses deep learning to achieve high accuracy in eukaryotic gene annotation, rivaling evidence-based methods.

papers.title: Long-lived retinal ganglion cells
papers.summary: Long-lived retinal ganglion cells were identified, but details of the study are not provided.

papers.title: Connectome-seq: high-throughput mapping of neuronal connectivity at single-synapse resolution via barcode sequencing
papers.authors: Danping Chen, Alina Isakova, Zhou Wan, Mark J. Wagner, Yunming Wu et al. (+1 more)
papers.summary: Connectome-seq is a method for high-throughput mapping of neuronal connectivity at single-synapse resolution, using RNA barcoding and sequencing.

papers.title: Highly accurate ab initio gene annotation with ANNEVO
papers.authors: Pengyu Zhang, Tun Xu, Songbo Wang, Xiaofei Yang, Peisen Sun et al. (+8 more)
papers.summary: ANNEVO is a deep learning approach for accurate and scalable ab initio gene annotation of diverse genomes, modeling sequence evolution and long-range dependencies.

papers.title: Pyroptosis-boosting nanoadjuvants enable effective in situ cancer vaccination
papers.summary: A nanoadjuvant platform was developed to induce pyroptosis in tumour cells, enabling effective in situ cancer vaccination and systemic tumour eradication.

papers.title: A nanoporous capacitive electrochemical ratchet for continuous ion separations
papers.authors: Rylan Kautz, Alon Herman, Ethan J. Heffernan, Keren Shushan Alshochat, Eden Grossman et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: A new device is demonstrated that can separate ions without using redox reactions, which could be useful for water desalination and other applications.

papers.title: Grain boundary stabilization of fluorite ferroelectrics
papers.authors: Shiyu Wang, Hai Zhong, Siyi Song, Ang Gao, Qinghua Zhang et al. (+4 more)
papers.summary: Researchers have found a way to stabilize certain types of materials that are useful for electronics, by controlling the arrangement of ions at their boundaries.

papers.title: Pathways to commercially viable organic photovoltaic materials
papers.authors: Jianhua Han, Han Xu, Daniel Corzo, Anirudh Sharma, Derya Baran
papers.summary: A review discusses the challenges and opportunities for developing low-cost, efficient, and stable materials for organic photovoltaics.

papers.title: Enriched membrane stackings made possible
papers.summary: A new method is reported for making thin layers of materials with specific symmetries, which could be useful for electronics and other applications.

papers.title: Prediction of rheological properties via structure elucidation of solvated hydrogels
papers.authors: Nathan D. Rosenmann, Lauren M. Irie, Joanna Korpanty, Eric W. Roth, Reiner Bleher et al. (+8 more)
papers.summary: Researchers have used a new microscopy technique to study the structure of hydrogels and understand how their properties are related to their structure.

papers.title: Unravel the complexity
papers.summary: Researchers used a hidden Markov model to analyze the resistance fluctuation data of a phase-change material called GeTe, revealing its complex free energy landscape and glass relaxation dynamics.

papers.title: Stress-relaxing granular bioprinting materials enable complex and uniform organoid self-organization
papers.authors: Austin J. Graham, Michelle W. L. Khoo, Vasudha Srivastava, Sara Viragova, Honesty Kim et al. (+19 more)
papers.summary: Scientists developed a tunable granular biomaterial matrix that supports long-term bioprinting and enables complex and uniform organoid self-organization through stress relaxation.

papers.title: Bringing magnetofluorescent proteins into the cell
papers.summary: Researchers developed a method to bring magnetofluorescent proteins into cells, but details of the method are not provided.

papers.title: Engineering gut biosensors
papers.summary: Scientists engineered gut biosensors, but details of the biosensors are not provided.

papers.title: Live imaging of neuronal dynamics in transparent mouse brains
papers.summary: Researchers developed an isotonic and minimally invasive optical clearing medium called SeeDB-Live, which enables deep-tissue live imaging of cellular functions ex vivo and in vivo.

papers.title: An update on spatial proteomics
papers.summary: This paper provides a non-comprehensive look back at the recent developments in spatial proteomics.

papers.title: Learning genes deeply
papers.summary: The authors present a deep learning method called Annevo that achieves high accuracy in eukaryotic gene annotation.

papers.title: Long-lived retinal ganglion cells
papers.summary: This paper discusses long-lived retinal ganglion cells, but no specific findings or significance are mentioned.

papers.title: Connectome-seq: high-throughput mapping of neuronal connectivity at single-synapse resolution via barcode sequencing
papers.authors: Danping Chen, Alina Isakova, Zhou Wan, Mark J. Wagner, Yunming Wu et al. (+1 more)
papers.summary: The authors present a new method called Connectome-seq that allows for high-throughput mapping of neuronal connectivity at single-synapse resolution.

papers.title: Highly accurate ab initio gene annotation with ANNEVO
papers.authors: Pengyu Zhang, Tun Xu, Songbo Wang, Xiaofei Yang, Peisen Sun et al. (+8 more)
papers.summary: The authors present a deep learning method called ANNEVO that advances accurate and scalable ab initio gene annotation of evolutionarily diverse genomes.
